questions.text: I am comfortable letting others make the final call.
I often take it upon myself to resolve group disagreements by choosing a path.
I tend to exhaust my own resources before asking others to contribute theirs.
I feel that my strength lies in sustaining group cohesion.
I routinely pick up tasks no one else wants to do, so the group can function well.
I sometimes feel overwhelmed by the sheer intensity of being alive.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering reveals the depth of my values in a way that ordinary life does not.
I find that I am at my most helpful when I can analyze a crisis without being pulled into its emotional current.
I notice that I can hold space for difficult conversations without my own equilibrium being disrupted.
I believe my emotional intensity is central to who I am.
I think it is important to make a stand and defend it.
I find that over-preparation can make me rigid and slow to adapt when things change.
I am content letting the group commit without needing to add my final word.
I detect when someone is saying yes but meaning no.
I hold the view that my system is designed for endurance rather than intensity.
I find that I am most trusted in difficult situations because I bring logical clarity rather than emotional intensity.
I tend not to detect when a friendly question is actually a form of gatekeeping.
I tend to be focused on my own train of thought during conversations rather than scanning for reactions.
I detect when someone uses a nickname versus a full name to signal the current state of a relationship.
I feel that my role in any friendship is to be the one who gives more.
I hold the view that the pull toward responding to suffering is the most honest expression of the values I actually hold.
I find that even significant life changes don't produce the emotional turbulence others describe.
I send emails as soon as I write them, trusting my first phrasing is good enough.
I prefer to own the relationship with external partners on behalf of the team.
I develop my argument as I speak, discovering what I think in real time.
I hold the view that the obligation to respond to suffering is among the most non-negotiable commitments a person can hold.
I track my goals with quantifiable metrics so I can see objective progress.
I prioritize long-term wellbeing over short-term pleasure when the two conflict.
I build buffers into estimates because I know unexpected complications are inevitable.
I am highly aware of who initiates conversation and who waits to be approached in social settings.
I have been told that I feel things more intensely than most people.
I hold the view that it is important to be my own biggest advocate.
I find that I have to work at reading social situations that others navigate effortlessly.
I routinely protect my focus time even when others express disappointment.
I think that approaching others' distress with calm, logical analysis is the behavior that most reliably converts good intentions into measurable improvements.
I feel that ensuring my own financial security comes before generosity.
